utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright thanks for coming in today i see on my chart here that you had a bunch of lower respiratory infections so first tell me how are you what's going on

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know i'm doing better now but you know last week i was really sick and i just have had enough like i was coughing a lot a lot of mucus even had some shortness of breath and even a low-grade fever

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wow that is a lot so what did you do for some of those symptoms

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know i ended up drinking a lot of fluid and taking some robitussin and i actually got better over the weekend and now i'm feeling much better but what concerns me is that i i tend to get pneumonia a lot

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so when you say a lot like how frequently does it occur i would say it seem honestly it seems like it's every month or every other month especially over the past six six months that i just keep getting sick and i usually will end up having to go to my primary care doctor or

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: urgent care and i'll get prescribed some antibiotics and one time i actually ended up in the emergency room

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wow and how long do your symptoms normally last for

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know it could be as few as like a couple of days but sometimes it could go even up to a week

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm you mentioned that you are a farmer did you do you notice that your symptoms occur while doing certain things on the farm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know i was trying to think about that and i've been working on the farm for some time but the only thing i can think about is that i've been helping my brother out and i've been started like unloading a lot of hay which i do n't usually do and i wan na say that my symptoms actually start the days that i'm unloading hay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright do you wear a mask when you're unloading hay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i do n't do that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: none of us do

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay yeah so like that your brother does n't either

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i'm the only one who seems to be getting sick

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright so i know you said you were trying to like help out your brother like what's going on with him

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know we've just been getting really busy and so he has been working around doing other things so i've just been helping him just cover the extra load

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm okay alright do you have any other siblings

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah there is actually ten of us

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wow okay that's that's a lot of siblings

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i'm okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: maybe maybe we could we could always stick them in they could get some work done the holidays must be fun at your place

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah we do n't need to hire any i mean have anyone else this is our family

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're right keep it in the family okay so speaking of family do you have do you or anyone have a history of seasonal allergies

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no no i have never had any problems with allergies

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and do you smoke

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't smoke

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: do you live with anybody who does

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do not

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so okay so now i i wan na go ahead and do my physical exam i'm gon na call out some of my findings just to make sure that i'm documenting everything and if you have any questions about what it is that i'm saying please feel free to ask okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so i reviewed your vitals and you appear to be breathing a little fast your respiratory rate is twenty but but your oxygen is you're satting kind of fine at ninety nine percent on room air so i'm not too worried about that on for on your heart exam i do you have a regular rate and regular rhythm i do not appreciate any murmurs rubs or gallops on your lung exam you know i do you do have some fine rales on your lung exam but no wheezes and on your musculoskeletal exam i do not appreciate any clubbing of your fingers so for your results i did review the results of your chest x-ray and i noticed some round glass opacities so let me tell you a little bit about like my assessment and plan for your first problem of recurrent lung infections your symptoms seem consistent with a condition we call hypersensitivity pneumonitis in your case another name is farmer's lung which you know is appropriate considering your job this could be caused by bacteria and or mold that is found in the hay when you inhale it it leads to an allergic reaction in your lungs this is why your symptoms occur every time you move hay for your current symptoms i'm gon na prescribe you a a course of an oral steroid this will help to decrease the inflammation that is occurring in your lungs i will also be ordering a cat scan of your lungs which will help confirm the diagnosis as as well a pulmonary function test to assess how severe your respiratory impairment is it would be best if you could eliminate your exposure to the hay or prevent further to prevent further damage to your lungs however if you are unable it's very important that you wear a respirator when moving hay around i know that that was a lot of information i think it boils down to pull in more of your siblings to help work around but do you have any questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah so is this gon na help so i do n't keep getting sick

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so ideally what we are doing i think this is the best course of action to deal with the deeper problem right of these infections and to kind of like clear up the pneumonia everything seems to hint on so what we're gon na do is treat your current infection we're going to either prevent you from being around hay or make it so that it's safe for you to be with hay and then we're gon na see like what we need to do moving forward does that help

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay it does

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay no problem

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: alright

detail_list.summary: Recurrent lung infections.
The patient reports shortness of breath, productive cough, and low-grade fever.
The patient expresses concern about the frequency of his pneumonia.
The doctor asks about the frequency of the patient's symptoms.
The doctor asks about the duration of the patient's symptoms.
The patient is a farmer.
The doctor asks if symptoms occur during specific farm activities.
The patient reports symptoms occur when he is unloading hay.
The doctor asks if the patient wears a mask while unloading hay.
The doctor asks about a history of seasonal allergies.
The patient denies a history of seasonal allergies.
The doctor asks if the patient smokes.
The patient denies smoking.
The doctor asks if the patient lives with anyone who smokes.
The patient denies living with anyone who smokes.
Physical exam findings include a respiratory rate of 20, 99% oxygen saturation, regular heart rhythm without murmurs, fine rales on lung auscultation, and no clubbing.
Chest X-ray shows round glass opacities.
The diagnosis is hypersensitivity pneumonitis caused by bacteria or mold in hay.
The doctor prescribes oral steroids, orders a CT scan of the lungs, and orders a pulmonary function test.
The doctor advises the patient to eliminate exposure to hay or wear a respirator.
The patient asks if the treatment plan will prevent future illness.
The doctor explains the treatment plan aims to resolve current infection and prevent future exposure to hay.
